attributes.stem:
A 45-year-old man presents to the emergency department after a vomit of about 500 ml of fresh blood. This was preceded with a six week history of intermittent upper abdominal pain, worse after meals. He drinks several glasses of wine most days and had a drinking binge three nights ago which culminated in a single large forceful vomit. This contained the meal he had just eaten, but no blood. Apart from a work-related back injury several months ago, his history is otherwise unremarkable. He took a course of diclofenac for the back pain.

On examination his blood pressure is 108/72 mmHg, pulse 98/min and temperature 37.0oC. The rest of the physical examination is unremarkable.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one the following is the most likely cause of the bleed?
attributes.explanation:
Based on the characteristic preceding epigastric pain and the large haematemesis, peptic ulcer disease (PUD) is the most likely explanation for the bleed. It is possible that his recent use of a non-steroidal anti-inflammatory agent could have been ulcerogenic. Peptic ulcer disease has a life-time prevalence of 5-10%, with the incidence declining world-wide (notably in high-income countries). Risk factors include Helicobacter pylori infection and NSAID use. Other less clear risk factors include smoking, alcohol intake and psychological stress. The severity of haematemesis in this patient suggests ongoing bleeding and requires prompt endoscopic intervention.

Boerhaaves syndrome, or oesophageal rupture, presents as a sudden onset of severe retrosternal chest pain after forceful retching or vomiting. Haematemesis is not a presenting symptom of Boerhaaves syndrome. Clinical features include subcutaneous or mediastinal emphysema. Upon cardiac auscultation, crackling may be heard. This is termed ‘Hamman’s sign’.

Mallory Weiss tearing occurs after multiple episodes of increased intra-abdominal pressure (e.g.: vomiting, coughing) and is characterised by longitudinal mucosal lacerations within the oesophagus. This syndrome occurs in 3-5% of all cases of haematemesis and is generally a benign and self-limiting disease process. Given this patient only experienced one episode of emesis, this is less likely. In addition, the vomiting associated with the binge drinking was three days ago - which makes an emetogenic tear less likely.

Oesophageal varices occur in patients with portal hypertension, which the patient had no signs of on clinical examination. This patient may have gastritis secondary to an alcohol binge, helicobacter pylori infection or immune mediated gastritis. The more likely presentation of gastritis is epigastric pain, nausea and vomiting, not haematemesis.
learningPoints: Helicobacter pylori infection and NSAIDs are the two most common causes of peptic ulcer disease
references: Lanas A, Chan FKL (2017). Peptic Ulcer Disease. Lancet, 390: 613-24
Malik UF, Young R, Pham HD, McCon A, Shen B, Landres R, Mahmoud A (2010). Chronic presentation of Boerhaave's syndrome. BMC Gastroenterology, 10 (29)
Feinman M, Haut ER. Upper gastrointestinal bleeding. Surgical Clinics. 2014 Feb 1;94(1):43-53.
